blocks.heading: The shared service that turns a d2 or mermaid diagram source into a rendered SVG — the render floor every generator stands on.

rows.value: 4 modules, no dependencies on the rest of the toolkit — a leaf at the bottom of the stack.
Compile diagram sources to SVG via the `d2` CLI, optionally upgrade flowchart-class diagrams to an editable Excalidraw scene, and own the **semantic color palette** every diagram speaks.

bigIdea.label: The load-bearing idea
bigIdea.line: "One palette drives BOTH the d2 prelude and the mermaid classDefs."
bigIdea.sub: `PALETTE` in `diagram-colors.ts` is the single source of truth. From it we generate the d2 `classes {}` block injected before every render **and** the matching mermaid `classDef` lines — so a `changed` node is the same amber whether it renders as a static d2 image or an editable mermaid→Excalidraw scene. Diagrams never drift between the two representations because there is only one place a color lives.

blocks.title: The pieces
blocks.intro: A renderer with a hard floor and an opt-in upgrade, fed by one palette and two source-shaping helpers (mermaid→d2 conversion and prose-fence promotion). Tap a card to jump to its full section.

cards.name: render-diagram
cards.purpose: The renderer. Compiles a diagram's d2 source to SVG via the external `d2` CLI (the floor), then — for flowchart-class diagrams only — optionally upgrades to an editable Excalidraw scene. A bad diagram degrades to a visible placeholder, never a broken document.

cards.name: palette
cards.purpose: The semantic color vocabulary and the single source of truth for it. `PALETTE` generates both the d2 `classes` prelude and the matching mermaid classDefs, plus the legend logic that lists only the roles actually applied.

cards.name: mermaid-to-d2
cards.purpose: A focused converter for the common mermaid flowchart subset → d2, so a mermaid-authored diagram still has a dependable d2 floor. Returns `null` for anything outside the subset rather than guessing.

cards.name: promote-mermaid
cards.purpose: Walks prose blocks and lifts each convertible ```mermaid fence into a real diagram block (d2 floor + mermaid source) so authored markdown gets rendered diagrams for free.

blocks.title: How a diagram renders
blocks.intro: A diagram source is either native d2 or mermaid. `renderDiagram` is the hub: it prepends the palette's `D2_CLASS_PRELUDE`, shells out to the external `d2` CLI for the SVG floor, and — only for eligible flowchart-class kinds — upgrades the mermaid source to an editable Excalidraw scene. `mermaidFlowchartToD2` is the converter branch that gives a mermaid-authored diagram its d2 floor.

diagram.caption: `renderDiagram` is the hub; the `d2` CLI is the only external dependency. The Excalidraw upgrade is opt-in and falls back to the d2 SVG on any error.

blocks.title: The pieces, in depth
blocks.intro: A full section per piece — what it does, how it works, its files and exports, and how it connects to the generators that consume it.

components.name: render-diagram
detail: `renderDiagram(block, opts, deps)` is the heart of the domain. It first compiles the block's **d2 source** to an SVG by writing `D2_CLASS_PRELUDE` + the source to a temp file and shelling out to the `d2` binary (`d2 --theme 0 --pad 24`). This is the dependable floor: it renders every diagram kind with no browser. If `d2` is missing or the source is invalid, the call degrades to a visible placeholder SVG (a warning box) rather than throwing — so one bad diagram never breaks a whole document.

The second stage is an **opt-in upgrade**. Routing is by diagram *kind*, not by toolchain availability: only `flowchart`, `architecture`, `sequence`, and `class` kinds are eligible, because those are the ones `mermaid-to-excalidraw` converts to native *editable* elements (ERD and stateDiagram silently rasterize, which is worse than the d2 sketch). When eligible and the opt-in Excalidraw toolchain is present, it converts the mermaid source to an editable `.excalidraw` scene plus a display SVG, forcing all text to dark `INK` for legibility. Any failure falls back to the d2 SVG already in hand — same aesthetic, never broken. `renderAll` maps this over many blocks in order.

files.name: render-diagram.ts
files.desc: The d2 floor, the placeholder fallback, and the opt-in Excalidraw upgrade path.

exports.name: renderDiagram()
exports.desc: → `DiagramResult` — compile one block to SVG (+ optional editable scene).

exports.name: DiagramResult · RenderOpts · RenderDeps
exports.desc: Result shape, render options (`outDir`/`excalidraw`/`onWarn`), and the injectable Excalidraw seam for tests.

connections.body: [palette](#c-palette) — `D2_CLASS_PRELUDE` is prepended to every d2 source; `INK` recolors Excalidraw text
the external `d2` binary on PATH (the only hard dependency)

[core](../domain-core/domain-core.html) render shell, [recap](../domain-recap/domain-recap.html), [atlas](../domain-atlas/domain-atlas.html), and [authoring](../domain-authoring/domain-authoring.html) — every generator that embeds a rendered diagram

components.name: palette
detail: `PALETTE` is the single source of truth for the six semantic roles (`changed`, `added`, `removed`, `actor`, `external`, `store`), each a `{ fill, stroke, bold? }`. From this one object two representations are generated so they can never drift: `D2_CLASS_PRELUDE` (the d2 `classes {}` block that `render-diagram` injects before every render, letting any source apply `class: <role>`) and `MERMAID_CLASSDEFS` (the matching `classDef` lines an editable flowchart includes so its Excalidraw scene stays colored). `INK` is the dark label color that guarantees readable contrast on every fill.

The legend logic keeps the rendered legend honest. `rolesInSource` scans a source for roles that are *applied* (`class: role` in d2, `:::role` or `class a,b role;` in mermaid) — excluding mere `classDef` definitions — and `legendRolesForRender` keys off the actual renderer: the d2 SVG carries d2 `class:` colors, but an Excalidraw scene carries only the mermaid classDefs, so a mermaid *sequence* (which has no class mechanism) renders colorless and correctly shows no legend even though its d2 floor defined roles.

files.name: diagram-colors.ts
files.desc: `PALETTE`, the generated d2 prelude + mermaid classDefs, `INK`, and the legend-role scanners.

exports.name: PALETTE · ColorRole · INK
exports.desc: The role→color map, the role union, and the dark label ink.

exports.name: D2_CLASS_PRELUDE · MERMAID_CLASSDEFS
exports.desc: The two generated representations of the palette — injected / included into sources.

exports.name: rolesInSource() · legendRolesForRender() · ROLE_LABELS
exports.desc: Which roles a source applies, which to legend per renderer, and human labels.

connections.body: [render-diagram](#c-render-diagram) — prelude prepended to every render; `INK` recolors Excalidraw text
[recap](../domain-recap/domain-recap.html), [atlas](../domain-atlas/domain-atlas.html), [authoring](../domain-authoring/domain-authoring.html) — every diagram recipe applies these role classes

components.name: mermaid-to-d2
detail: `mermaidFlowchartToD2(mermaid)` translates the common mermaid flowchart subset into equivalent d2, so a mermaid-authored diagram still has the dependable d2 render floor. It parses the `graph`/`flowchart` header for direction, walks each edge line splitting on the mermaid arrow forms (`-->`, `-.->`, `---`, `==>`), captures bracketed node labels and pipe edge labels, and emits `direction:`, node, and edge lines with everything quoted for safety.

Crucially it returns **`null`** for anything outside the subset (a missing header, an unparseable node shape) rather than emitting a half-broken diagram — the caller is expected to leave such a fence as inline code. That null-or-clean contract is what makes [promote-mermaid](#c-promote-mermaid) safe to run over arbitrary authored prose.

files.name: mermaid-to-d2.ts
files.desc: The mermaid-flowchart-subset → d2 parser (header, nodes, edges) with a strict null fallback.

exports.name: mermaidFlowchartToD2()
exports.desc: → `string | null` — d2 source, or null when the mermaid is outside the supported subset.

connections.body: [promote-mermaid](#c-promote-mermaid) — to give each promoted fence its d2 floor

components.name: promote-mermaid
detail: `promoteMermaidFences(blocks)` lets authored markdown carry diagrams for free. It walks prose blocks and, for each fenced ```mermaid block, calls [mermaidFlowchartToD2](#c-mermaid-to-d2). When conversion succeeds it splits the prose, emitting the surrounding text as prose blocks and the fence as a real `diagram` block (kind `flowchart`, carrying both the converted d2 floor and the original mermaid source for the editable upgrade). When conversion returns `null` the fence is left untouched inline, so nothing is ever rendered half-broken.

This is the bridge from human-written markdown into the same render pipeline the structured generators use — the same `DiagramBlock` shape `render-diagram` consumes, so a hand-written mermaid diagram and a programmatically authored one render identically.

files.name: promote-mermaid.ts
files.desc: Prose-block walker that lifts convertible ```mermaid fences into diagram blocks.

exports.name: promoteMermaidFences()
exports.desc: → `Block[]` — prose blocks with convertible mermaid fences promoted to diagram blocks.

connections.body: [mermaid-to-d2](#c-mermaid-to-d2) for the d2 floor of each promoted fence

blocks.title: Data it owns
blocks.intro: Diagrams owns the **semantic role vocabulary** — the six color roles every diagram across the toolkit speaks. `PALETTE` is the single source; the d2 prelude and mermaid classDefs are generated from it.

rows.name: changed
rows.desc: Modified / the subject — bold amber (`#ffd43b`/`#f08c00`). The one role drawn with extra stroke weight so the subject pops.

rows.name: external
rows.desc: Third-party system — gray (`#f1f3f5`/`#adb5bd`). The `d2` CLI itself is tagged this in the arch diagram.

blocks.note: Roles are *applied* in sources (`class: <role>` in d2, `class X <role>;` in mermaid), never redefined — sources must not author their own top-level `classes:` block, since d2 silently merges it and would let a recipe shadow the canonical palette.

blocks.title: Seams
blocks.intro: A foundation leaf: it exposes a render API and a color vocabulary, and depends on nothing in the toolkit — only the external `d2` binary.

exposes.note: compile diagram blocks to SVG (+ optional editable scene)
lift mermaid fences in prose to diagrams

blocks.note: No meaningful internal dependencies — this is the foundation layer. The only runtime requirement is the external `d2` binary on PATH; when it is absent, `renderDiagram` degrades to a visible placeholder rather than failing. Consumers: [core](../domain-core/domain-core.html), [recap](../domain-recap/domain-recap.html), [atlas](../domain-atlas/domain-atlas.html), and [authoring](../domain-authoring/domain-authoring.html) all render through it.
